text.content:
Source-backed journal smoke test.

This entry proves the Anime 5e core rules reference pack can be populated through the same source manifest/import workflow as Items and Actors. Full rules references are added in later #34 slices.

Source: Anime 5E Fifth Edition Core Rules, PDF p. 20.

  1. Agree on campaign tone, party needs, and starting level with the DM.
  2. Record starting XP from the level benchmark and apply all skipped level benefits if beginning above 1st level.
  3. Set the starting Discretionary Point budget, then pay for Ability Scores and Species/Race choices.
  4. Choose a Class, then add Attributes, Defects, proficiencies, resources, equipment, and notes on the character sheet.
  5. Use the sheet point summary as the working audit trail: it should show available points, spent points, refunds, and remaining points.

Source: Anime 5E Fifth Edition Core Rules, PDF p. 22; Character Folio PDF p. 6.

Start with the DM-approved Discretionary Point budget. The system's point summary treats Ability Scores, Species/Race, Size Templates, Classes, Attributes, powers, techniques, Items of Power, and manual spending as costs. Defects, manual refunds, class-derived Bonus Points, Engagement Bonus Points, and other non-levelling awards increase available points.

Negative remaining points mean the build is overspent. Resolve that by lowering costs, adding approved Defect refunds, adding awarded points, or marking the build as a deliberate manual exception in notes.

documents.name: Species Classes Attributes and Defects Workflow

text.content:
Source: Anime 5E Fifth Edition Core Rules, PDF pp. 28, 44-48, 50, 92, 133.

Species/Race entries spend or return points through their trait package and may add ability modifiers, languages, movement, Attributes, or Defects. Applying a Species item lets the sheet derive ability modifiers and sync species-granted Attributes and Defects as managed owned Items.

Size Templates describe non-Medium body scale. Smaller templates return points and larger templates cost points; the template also records lift/carry, Strength, attack, Armour Class, inflicted damage, received standard damage, and range/speed modifiers from Table 05.

Race/Class combinations have no hard prerequisites in Anime 5E. Table 06 marks combinations as Rare, Common, or Preferred to guide tone, physical fit, synergy, and narrative likelihood; DM approval and sufficient Points remain the real requirements.

Class items carry level, hit die, proficiencies, level progression, and class-derived Bonus Points. Multiclass builds expose duplicate benefit and reallocation review rows.

Attributes, powers, and techniques spend points by rank and can be modified by Enhancements or Limiters. Defects return points by rank and should describe the limitation clearly enough for the DM to apply it in play.

Source: Anime 5E Fifth Edition Core Rules, PDF pp. 52, 183-184.

Track current XP, current level, next threshold, and XP-to-next-level on the character sheet. Table 08 gives the level thresholds and proficiency bonus progression through 20th level.

XP can come from overcoming conflict, completing meaningful tasks, learning from failure, or other campaign events. Engagement Bonus Points and other non-levelling awards can be recorded separately from XP when the reward should improve the character without changing level immediately.

When a character crosses the next XP threshold, add the new Class level, update benefits, and review unspent Bonus Points or saved allocation notes before the next session.

Source: Anime 5E Fifth Edition Core Rules, PDF pp. 152-155.

Roll only when the result is uncertain and the outcome matters. Most checks use d20 + the relevant Ability modifier, plus Proficiency Bonus when a Skill, tool, Saving Throw, weapon, or other proficiency applies.

  • Difficulty Class: compare the check total to the DM-set DC; DC 15 is the normal medium benchmark and the default Attribute activation DC unless the DM changes it.
  • Contest: opposing characters roll against one another; the higher check total wins and a tie usually leaves the situation unchanged.
  • Advantage or disadvantage: roll two d20s and keep the higher or lower die. Multiple advantages or disadvantages do not add extra dice, and mixed advantage/disadvantage cancels out.
  • Passive checks: with DM approval, use 10 instead of rolling; use 15 with advantage or 5 with disadvantage.
  • Common roll types: Ability checks, Skill checks, Saving Throws, Initiative rolls, attack rolls, and damage rolls.

documents.name: Action and Combat Flow Quick Reference

text.content:
Source: Anime 5E Fifth Edition Core Rules, PDF pp. 159-162, 171.

Combat starts by rolling Initiative once for the scene unless the table uses the dynamic Initiative option. Initiative is a Dexterity check, then turns resolve from highest total to lowest each round.

  1. On a turn, take one main action; Extra Actions and certain class features can add Bonus Actions.
  2. Choose an attack action, tactical action, or general action. Attack actions resolve attacks, tactical actions support later attacks or defense, and general actions cover everything else the DM adjudicates.
  3. For an attack, declare the method, target, weapon or special attack, and any unusual intent before rolling.
  4. Attack roll = d20 + the relevant Ability modifier + Proficiency Bonus when proficient. Meet or beat the target's Armour Class to hit.
  5. Apply situational advantages, disadvantages, range penalties, cover, flanking, total defence, called shots, and similar modifiers before resolving damage.

Use the actor sheet's HP, Energy, weapons, armour, and owned Items as the table-facing state while the DM adjudicates unusual actions.

Source: Anime 5E Fifth Edition Core Rules, PDF pp. 161-168, 171.

Combat manoeuvres are optional Core Rules procedures for attack actions and tactical actions. The actor sheet can post these references to chat or roll a selected common attack with the manoeuvre's advantage or disadvantage guidance applied.

  • Tactical actions: Aim and Wait for Opening prepare advantage for a later ranged or melee attack. Total Defence forces attackers to roll at disadvantage.
  • Grappling: Grapple uses an unarmed attack to grab instead of damage. Later attacks can Lock, Throw, Pin, or attempt to disarm by grabbing the weapon.
  • Called shots: Disarm, Vital Spot, Weak Point, Specific Location, and Knock Out are declared before the attack and usually impose disadvantage unless a relevant Combat Technique removes it.
  • Attack manoeuvres: Two-Weapon Fighting, Multiple Targets, Strike to Wound, Total Attack, Surprise Attack, Touch Target, and Mounted Fast Attack change attack roll mode, damage handling, target AC, or defensive risk.
  • Modifiers: Advantage and disadvantage cancel each other and do not stack. Range penalties, cover, flanking, total attack, total defence, and target state should be reviewed before damage is resolved.

documents.name: Damage Recovery and Energy Quick Reference

text.content:
Source: Anime 5E Fifth Edition Core Rules, PDF pp. 174-178.

Successful attacks, hazards, and special effects subtract damage from current Hit Points. Weapon damage normally uses the listed damage dice plus the same Ability modifier used for the attack roll; size modifiers and Attribute effects may also apply.

  • Damage types: damage labels such as acid, fire, force, piercing, poison, psychic, slashing, standard, and thunder mainly matter when another rule refers to resistance, vulnerability, protection, or a specific effect.
  • Zero HP: a character at 0 HP or below is unconscious from a severe wound. Optional death Saving Throws can track whether they stabilize or die.
  • Wound pressure: the DM may impose disadvantage on d20 rolls when a character is at one-quarter maximum HP or lower.
  • Short rest: after at least one hour of light activity, spend Hit Dice to recover HP.
  • Long rest: after at least eight hours, recover all HP and regain spent Hit Dice up to half the character's total.
  • Energy: optional resource for Dynamic Powers, Spell-Like Ability, Deplete effects, fatigue, dramatic feats, and player retcons. Characters recover 1d8 Energy after a short rest and all Energy after a long rest.
  • Dramatic Feats: player characters and significant NPCs can spend 5 Energy per +1 bonus after a d20 roll in moments of high drama.
  • Player Retcon: with DM permission, minor plot changes cost 10 Energy and major plot changes cost 50 Energy; major changes are normally limited to Level 8+ or Energised characters.

Source: Anime 5E Fifth Edition Core Rules, PDF p. 180.

Deprivation covers lack of food, sleep, rest, shelter, water, air, and similar life-sustaining essentials. Appropriate Features, Immunity, or Resilient may prevent a specific deprivation. Otherwise, the character loses 1 Hit Point every listed interval, and that Hit Point loss is incurable until the deprivation ends.

TypeHit Point Loss Interval
Starvation1 per 8 hours
Hunger (minimal food)1 per day
Inadequate Nutrition1 per week
Total Dehydration1 per 2 hours
Partial Dehydration1 per 8 hours
Overheating1 per 4 hours
Freezing Cold1 per hour
Freezing Water1 per minute
Drowning1 per second

The Anime 5e actor sheet tracks Deprivation Loss on Hit Points. Applying deprivation reduces current HP and maximum HP; recovering deprivation removes the cap reduction but does not heal HP automatically.

Source: Anime 5E Fifth Edition Core Rules, PDF pp. 192-205.

Anime 5E treats important custom gear as effect-built Items. If two effects create equivalent combat or story impact, they should usually cost the same number of Points even when one is a physical object and the other is an innate power.

  • Item Attribute: grants 5 construction Points per Rank while costing 4 Points per Rank because Items can be lost, stolen, broken, or restricted by circumstances.
  • Mundane items: common gear, weapons, armour, lodging, and adventuring supplies can be free when the DM treats them as ordinary equipment. Spending Points on an otherwise mundane Item can make it conceptually protected.
  • Point protection: Point-costed Items are part of the character concept and should usually be restored or replaced soon when lost, unless doing so would break the story.
  • Weapons: use the weapon tables as examples or templates. Adjust Rank, Enhancements, Limiters, damage type, range, and point cost to model custom attacks.
  • Armour and shields: record armour category, proficiency expectations, AC formula, Strength or Stealth limits, shield size, hands used, and weight.
  • Crafting projects: track target Item, status, materials, Point cost, progress, required progress, DC, linked Item UUID, and notes until the DM resolves completion.

Source: Anime 5E Fifth Edition Core Rules, PDF pp. 250-253.

The DM adjudicates rules questions, table tone, pacing, and unusual applications of Anime 5E's open-ended point framework. When a rule is unclear, make a temporary call, keep play moving, and revisit the ruling after the session if the table needs a lasting precedent.

  • Rules questions: use Ability or Skill checks for most uncertain situations, and skip rolls when the outcome is obvious or the story is better served by a direct ruling.
  • House rules: record changes, discuss them with players before they matter at the table, and watch for Point-balance side effects.
  • Damage control: correct mistakes openly, address player conflict out of character, and redirect scenes without making player time feel wasted.
  • Power abuse: ask for redesigns or table limits when an interpretation breaks the campaign tone or overwhelms other characters.
  • DM craft: use strong names, recurring locations, visible options, safe boundaries, meaningful spotlight moments, and consistent rulings.

Source: Anime 5E Fifth Edition Core Rules, PDF p. 257.

Aradia is a romantic-fantasy living world: a beautiful, mostly pastoral planet whose people interpret the planet's will through signs in weather and sky. It supports angelic Asrai, speaking animal companions, rescued Fairies, emissary traditions, and a military host formed after demonic incursion.

Use Aradia for campaigns about guardianship, healing other worlds, lost celestial heirs, emissary missions, corruption contained but not cured, and moral fantasy that still leaves room for danger.

Source: Anime 5E Fifth Edition Core Rules, PDF pp. 258-259.

Bazaroth is a horror-fantasy dying world locked between a burning dayside and frozen darkside. Hellspawn societies, demonic aristocracies, volcanic underworlds, mutation jungles, ghost-haunted borderlands, and brutal rites of passage make it a setting for survival, corruption, infernal politics, and monstrous expeditions.

Use Bazaroth when the campaign needs harsh environmental threats, demon lineages, dark bargains, volcanic fortresses, psychic predation, or parties trying to escape or change a world that punishes mercy.

Source: Anime 5E Fifth Edition Core Rules, PDF pp. 259-261.

Ikaris is a high-fantasy sword-and-sorcery world built around a vast supercontinent, mage-ruled successor states, magical vows, border monsters, nomad steppes, ruined underground realms, and the coastal Land of Seven Stars.

Use Ikaris for traditional fantasy campaigns with Anime 5E's point-based character options: feuding magical nobles, oath-breaking warlocks, monster-haunted marches, guild politics, martial orders, and celestial or demonic legends under active political pressure.

Source: Anime 5E Fifth Edition Core Rules, PDF p. 262.

Gedanken is an optional thought experiment for groups that want Ability Scores to be expressed on a cleaner positive-number scale instead of relying on legacy 3-18 values and derived modifiers.

  • Convert an old Ability Score by subtracting 10, dividing by 2, rounding toward zero when negative, then adding 6.
  • Because the new values run higher than traditional modifiers, raise Difficulty Classes by about 5 when using the revised scale.
  • Adjust related assumptions carefully: Ability Score point costs, base Armour Class, attack damage modifiers, and any rule that expects the old score/modifier split.
  • Use this only as a deliberate campaign mode; do not mix it casually with the default character sheet math.

Source: Anime 5E Fifth Edition Core Rules, PDF p. 263.

Anime 5E can share a table with traditional Fifth Edition material, but conversions should preserve the character concept while watching for Point balance, action economy, and option scope.

  • Races and species: Anime 5E traits can usually become features; imported Fifth Edition traits can map to Attributes, Defects, or Unique Attributes.
  • Classes: imported classes may need Bonus Points from the class deconstruction guidance to stay comparable with point-built Anime 5E characters.
  • Attributes: Dynamic Powers, Enhanced Proficiency, Extra Actions, and Skill Proficiency need special scrutiny because they may not fit the tighter assumptions of standard Fifth Edition.
  • Rules modules: called shots, tactical actions, grid movement, encumbrance, and monster CR can be adopted selectively if the table agrees on the mode.

documents.name: Character Sheet Help - Overview and Creation

text.content:
Source: Anime 5E Fifth Edition Core Rules, PDF p. 272; Anime 5e Character Folio sheet.

Use the Overview tab as the character's front page: Ability Scores, modifiers, HP, Energy, Armour Class, Proficiency Bonus, movement, identity, level, XP, and class rows all live there.

  • Enter starting level and starting XP in Creation Setup, then use the Apply Start action to sync the character's level and XP benchmark.
  • Use Source Budget and XP Benchmark as quick checks against the Core Rules starting assumptions.
  • Watch the point summary and validation notes before play; negative remaining points or warnings usually mean an owned Item, Species, Class, Attribute, Defect, or manual award needs review.
  • Use the roll buttons beside abilities when the table needs a basic d20 check from the sheet.

Source: Anime 5E Fifth Edition Core Rules, PDF pp. 272-273; Anime 5e Character Folio sheet.

The build tabs collect owned Items that define what the character is: Species, Size Templates, Classes, Attributes, Defects, Skills, tools, languages, powers, spells, psionics, and techniques.

  • Drop source-backed Items onto the actor or create new Items from the tab actions.
  • Attributes and Defects should carry ranks, Point costs or refunds, source notes, and any Enhancement or Limiter references needed for point accounting.
  • Class and Species Items should stay on the actor so derived benefits, warnings, and point totals remain visible.
  • Use notes areas for activation rules, ranges, targets, Energy costs, preparation limits, and table rulings that are too specific for a short Item name.

documents.name: Character Sheet Help - Combat Powers and Inventory

text.content:
Source: Anime 5E Fifth Edition Core Rules, PDF pp. 272-273; Anime 5e Character Folio sheet.

Use the Combat, Powers, and Inventory areas during play. Combat summarizes attacks, damage, defenses, movement, and active effects; Powers holds Energy-using and special abilities; Inventory holds gear, wallet values, Items of Power, weapons, armour, shields, loot, materials, and crafting projects.

  • Use item action icons to post notes, roll checks, roll attacks, roll damage, equip gear, edit Items, or delete Items.
  • Record explicit Energy costs and activation notes on powers when the source text or table ruling matters.
  • Equip weapons, armour, shields, and gear that should affect derived totals or table-facing state.
  • Use crafting projects to track target Item, status, material costs, progress, required progress, DC, linked Item UUID, and DM notes.

documents.name: Character Sheet Help - Biography Journal and Advancement

text.content:
Source: Anime 5E Fifth Edition Core Rules, PDF pp. 272-273; Anime 5e Character Folio sheet.

Use the Biography and Journal tabs for the parts of play that are not just numbers: appearance, personality, history, allies, enemies, goals, secrets, campaign role, session notes, rewards, downtime, and story events.

  • Advancement Bookkeeping shows current level, current XP, XP to next level, next threshold, Engagement Bonus Points, other non-levelling awards, and point-summary rows.
  • Record poisons, diseases, hazards, deprivation, and similar ongoing problems as Adventuring Risk Items so they can be posted to chat when they matter.
  • Keep advancement notes and reward notes separate from biography notes; it makes later audits of XP, Bonus Points, and story awards much easier.
  • Use Companions for vehicles, mecha, mounts, linked creatures, and owned equipment summaries that are too large for ordinary inventory rows.
